title: Clusia comans
taxonomy: Clusia comans is a member of the family Clusiaceae, commonly known as the St. John's Wort family. This family is part of the larger order Malpighiales. The order Malpighiales is a member of the subclass Rosidae, which is one of the two main subclasses of the class Magnoliopsida, or flowering plants. Within the class Magnoliopsida, Clusia comans is also classified as a dicot, or a plant with two distinct types of leaves. The classification of Clusia comans reflects its evolutionary relationships with other flowering plants.
uses: Clusia comans has been used in traditional medicine for its purported benefits, including treating various ailments and conditions. Its leaves, bark, and roots are said to possess anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant properties, which may contribute to its medicinal uses. In some cultures, Clusia comans is used to treat fever, rheumatism, and skin conditions. Additionally, its extracts have shown potential in reducing oxidative stress and inflammation, making it a subject of interest in modern medicine. Further research is necessary to fully understand the efficacy and safety of Clusia comans for medicinal purposes.

side_effects: Clusia comans is known to have potential side effects due to its toxicity. The sap of the plant contains a compound called saponin, which can be irritating to the skin and eyes. Ingestion of the plant can cause gastrointestinal issues, including nausea and vomiting.
